title: DevOps — Revision Log
description: Every subtopic from your DevOps/Infra prep guide, tracked the same way as your HLD log. Press Revised on a topic each time you go over it — the dot ages from green to red. Click into any subtopic for a deeper note and a reference link.

subs.d: VPCs (and any peered/transit-connected VPCs) need non-overlapping CIDR ranges, or routing between them becomes ambiguous. Planning address space up front — reserving room per environment/region — avoids painful re-IP-ing later as the account grows.

Public subnets host internet-facing components (ALBs, NAT Gateways); private subnets host application nodes and database clusters that should never be directly reachable from the internet. This separation is the first thing a reviewer checks in a VPC diagram.

Private subnets route outbound traffic through a highly-available NAT Gateway (one per AZ, ideally) so app servers can reach the internet (e.g. for package installs) without accepting any inbound connection initiated from outside.

ASGs scale instance count using target-tracking or step policies against CPU utilization, request count per target, or memory pressure (via custom CloudWatch metrics, since EC2 doesn't expose memory natively).
Running application nodes across multiple physical AZs means a single data-center-level failure doesn't take the whole service down — the standard baseline expectation for any production deployment.
Mixing Spot instances (cheap, reclaimable with ~2 min notice) for fault-tolerant workloads with on-demand for critical paths optimizes cost without risking availability where it matters.
Graviton nodes often give 20–40% better price/performance for compatible workloads, but require multi-arch container images and verifying all dependencies actually build for ARM.
Buckets should default to fully private, with access granted explicitly via bucket policy/IAM — public buckets are one of the most common real-world cloud misconfigurations.

Lifecycle rules expire/transition objects automatically (e.g. move to Glacier after 90 days); cross-region replication (CRR) improves durability and gives lower-latency reads to users in other regions.

Large files are split into parts uploaded independently (and potentially in parallel); if a connection drops mid-transfer, only the failed part needs to be retried instead of restarting the whole upload.

One stage compiles/builds with a full toolchain image; the final stage copies only the compiled artifact into a minimal runtime base (Alpine or scratch), keeping the shipped image small and reducing attack surface.

Ordering Dockerfile instructions from least-changing (installing dependencies) to most-changing (copying application code) maximizes cache reuse across local and CI builds, since Docker invalidates a layer — and everything after it — the moment its inputs change.
Running the container process as a restricted, non-root user (via USER in the Dockerfile) limits the blast radius if the container is ever compromised — a baseline hardening step reviewers look for.

Compose spins up app, database, and cache containers together with a shared network and env-var injection, letting a whole local stack start with one command instead of manually wiring containers together.
Named volumes persist stateful data (e.g. a local Postgres container's data directory) across container restarts and rebuilds — without one, restarting a DB container wipes its data.
Custom Docker networks let dependent services (app, DB, cache) resolve each other by container name and stay isolated from unrelated containers on the host.
Pushing images to Docker Hub or ECR tagged with precise semantic versions (not just latest) makes rollbacks and reproducible deployments possible.

Tools like Trivy or Grype (and ECR's built-in scanning) check images for known CVEs in OS packages and dependencies before they reach production — usually wired directly into the CI pipeline as a gate.

The API server validates and persists every change to cluster state into etcd, a highly-available distributed key-value store — every other control-plane component reads/writes through the API server, never etcd directly.

Controllers continuously compare actual cluster state to the declared desired state and act to close the gap (e.g. spinning up a replacement pod). The scheduler's specific job is assigning newly-created pods to healthy, resource-appropriate nodes.

Kubelet runs on every node and executes the container specs the scheduler assigned to it, reporting status back to the API server; kube-proxy maintains the iptables/IPVS rules that implement Service networking across the cluster.
A Pod is one or more tightly-coupled containers sharing a network namespace, storage volumes, and localhost loopback — containers inside it can talk to each other over localhost.

Deployments manage ReplicaSets for stateless workloads where any replica is interchangeable. StatefulSets give each replica a stable, persistent identity (hostname, storage) and start/terminate them in a strict order — needed for databases and other workloads where "which pod" matters.

A DaemonSet ensures exactly one copy of a pod runs on every (or every matching) node — the standard pattern for log/metric collection agents. CronJobs run one-off Job pods on a schedule for maintenance/batch tasks.

ConfigMaps inject non-sensitive config as environment variables or mounted files; Secrets do the same for sensitive values, base64-encoded at rest by default (not encrypted — pair with envelope encryption or an external secret store for real protection).

Helm packages a set of Kubernetes manifests into a versioned, parameterized "chart," so the same chart can be deployed to dev/staging/prod with different values instead of hand-editing YAML per environment.

ClusterIP is internal-only cluster communication; NodePort exposes a static port on every node's IP; LoadBalancer provisions a cloud load balancer for external ingress — each is a strictly wider-scope version of the one before it.
Ingress resources define host- and path-based routing rules so many services can share one entrypoint/load balancer instead of each needing its own — e.g. /api to one service, /admin to another.

A service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) injects a sidecar proxy alongside each pod to transparently handle mutual TLS, retries, and fine-grained traffic splitting (e.g. canary weighting) without changing application code.

Liveness probes trigger a restart if a container is stuck/deadlocked; readiness probes pause traffic to a pod until it's actually ready to serve; startup probes give slow-booting apps extra grace time before liveness checks kick in and kill them prematurely.

Requests reserve a minimum amount of CPU/memory for scheduling purposes; limits cap the maximum a container can consume — without both set correctly, a single noisy pod can starve its neighbors on the same node.

The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler adjusts replica count based on CPU/memory utilization, or external metrics like queue depth (via custom/external metrics adapters) — the pod-level counterpart to node-level autoscaling.
Karpenter watches for unschedulable pods and provisions the most cost-efficient node shape directly, reacting faster and more flexibly than a fixed-node-group cluster autoscaler.
Almost always an application-level failure — check kubectl logs --previous on the crashed container first, since the current logs may be empty right after a restart.
Usually a registry credential or image-tag problem — check the pod's image pull secrets and confirm the exact tag actually exists in the registry.
A task definition declares containers, ports, and resource needs (AWS's equivalent of a pod spec); an ECS Service keeps a desired task count running and wires it into ALB target groups.

Fargate abstracts away the underlying instances entirely — you pay per task's vCPU/memory and never patch a host — trading host-level control and often some cost efficiency for operational simplicity.
Feature-branch workflows isolate in-progress work; release-tracking branches stabilize a version before shipping; mandatory pull-request review gates catch issues before they hit the main branch.

Merging preserves full history with an explicit merge commit showing when branches joined. Rebasing replays your commits on top of the latest main branch, producing a clean linear history — but rewrites commit hashes, which is dangerous on shared/public branches.
Combining a feature branch's many "wip" / "fix typo" commits into one clean commit before merging keeps the main branch history readable and easy to bisect later.
Maven manages dependency resolution, compiles source, and runs test frameworks through phases (validate, compile, test, package, verify, install, deploy) declared in pom.xml.

Jenkinsfiles (Groovy-based, declarative or scripted) or GitHub Actions YAML workflows trigger build/test/package steps automatically on every push or PR — the backbone of "every commit is validated" CI.

Built artifacts (jars, Docker images, npm packages) get versioned and stored in an artifact repository rather than rebuilt from source every time — speeding up CI and giving every deployment a traceable, reproducible artifact.

Instances are updated in sequential batches while the rest keep serving traffic — minimal infrastructure cost, but rollback is slow (requires a full re-deploy) and old/new versions must stay API- and DB-compatible throughout the rollout.

Two identical environments exist simultaneously; the new version deploys to the idle one, gets validated, then a single load-balancer switch flips all traffic over — rollback is just switching back. Costs roughly double the infrastructure, which is why it's reserved for mission-critical systems like payment portals.

A small slice of production traffic (e.g. 2%) hits the new version first; if health metrics stay clean, the rollout expands gradually. Moderate infra overhead, fast rollback (just reduce traffic share or flip a flag), but both versions run live at once — so backward compatibility still matters.

Expand: add the new column/table alongside the old one. Migrate: backfill/copy existing data into the new shape while both old and new code paths still work. Contract: once every consumer has cut over, drop the legacy column — avoiding a hard schema lock that would break a live rolling/canary deployment mid-flight.

Instead of a CI job pushing changes to the cluster, a GitOps controller inside the cluster continuously pulls the desired state from a Git repo and reconciles live state to match — Git becomes the single source of truth, and every change is inherently auditable via commit history.

HCL describes the desired infrastructure state (not the imperative steps to get there); the same language and workflow apply across AWS, GCP, Azure, and dozens of other providers via their respective Terraform providers.

Terraform infers most dependencies automatically from resource references (e.g. a subnet referencing a VPC ID); depends_on expresses dependencies that aren't visible through direct attribute references.

The state file maps every resource in your .tf configuration to its real-world counterpart (by ID) — it's how terraform plan knows what already exists and what needs to change, and losing it effectively orphans your infrastructure from your code.

Storing state in a versioned S3 bucket lets a whole team share it safely; a DynamoDB table implements state locking so two people running apply at the same time can't corrupt the state file with concurrent writes.

Resource attributes marked sensitive are hidden from CLI output, but they still land in the state file in plaintext by default — real protection means encrypting the backend itself (S3 SSE) and tightly restricting who can read it.

A module bundles a set of related resources (e.g. "a VPC with subnets and route tables") behind clean input variables and outputs, so the same well-tested pattern can be reused across projects instead of copy-pasted.

Workspaces let one configuration manage multiple state files (one per environment) from the same code — simple, but risks accidental cross-environment applys. Separate folders/repos per environment are more explicit and safer for prod, at the cost of some duplication.

Infrastructure changed outside Terraform (a manual console tweak) causes state to "drift" from reality; running terraform plan regularly (or in CI) surfaces this drift before it causes a surprising apply.

Lightweight agents collect raw log lines from disk and normalize them into structured JSON (parsing timestamps, log levels, and custom fields via grok patterns) before forwarding — structured logs are what make later search/aggregation fast.
Structured logs are indexed (inverted index under the hood) for low-latency full-text search and aggregation across potentially billions of log lines.
Dashboards built on top of the indexed logs turn raw search results into diagnostic views — error-rate trends, top failing endpoints, request tracing views for on-call engineers.

Prometheus pulls metrics over HTTP from each target's /metrics endpoint at a configured interval, rather than services pushing metrics to it — this pull model makes it easy to tell when a target has gone silent (a missed scrape is itself a signal).
Grafana queries Prometheus (and other data sources) to build dashboards for infra utilization, request rates, and error rates — usually the first screen an on-call engineer opens during an incident.

Alertmanager evaluates rules like "P99 latency > 500ms for 5 minutes" and routes the resulting alert to the right channel (PagerDuty, Slack, email) — with grouping/deduplication so one root cause doesn't page the team 50 times.
A Lambda triggered directly by new CloudWatch Logs entries can filter for security events or reformat logs asynchronously, without a standing server dedicated to log processing.

A trace ID injected at the edge (the first point of entry) propagates through every downstream service call, letting tools like Jaeger/X-Ray/OpenTelemetry reconstruct the full request path and pinpoint exactly which microservice hop added the latency.
Static assets (images, JS bundles, video segments) are cached at globally distributed edge locations, so most requests never reach the origin servers at all — cutting both latency and backend load.
Terminating TLS at the CDN/edge boundary moves the compute-heavy cryptographic handshake off backend application servers, freeing them to spend CPU on actual application logic.
Automated rate limiting and traffic filtering at the edge absorb large-scale volumetric attacks before they ever reach origin infrastructure — AWS Shield on AWS, Google Cloud Armor on GCP.
Blocks SQL injection, XSS, and other application-layer attack patterns using signature and rate-based rules, sitting in front of the application at the CDN/load-balancer layer.

Token Bucket allows short bursts up to a capped bucket size, refilling at a steady rate — good for bursty-but-legitimate traffic. Leaky Bucket enforces a strictly constant outflow rate regardless of burstiness — good for smoothing traffic hitting a fragile downstream system.
Vault stores, encrypts, and can automatically rotate API keys, database credentials, and other secrets, issuing short-lived dynamic credentials instead of long-lived static ones wherever possible.

Centralizing authentication/authorization behind an OAuth 2.0-based identity provider means every internal tool trusts one login instead of maintaining its own credential store — see the HLD security module for the deeper OAuth flow breakdown.

Permissions are granted to roles (not individuals directly), and each role gets the minimum access needed to do its job — the standard mechanism (in Kubernetes RBAC, AWS IAM, etc.) for keeping a breach in one area from cascading everywhere.

An SLI is a measured indicator (e.g. request success rate); an SLO is a target for it (e.g. 99.9% success over 30 days); the error budget is the allowed room to fail (0.1%) — once it's spent, teams should prioritize reliability work over new features.
SLAs are the externally-facing, often contractual promises (with financial penalties for breach) built on top of internal SLOs, which are usually set stricter than the SLA to leave a safety margin.

A healthy on-call setup has clear rotation schedules, tiered escalation (if the primary doesn't ack in N minutes, page the secondary), and runbooks so responding doesn't depend on one specific engineer's memory.

A blameless postmortem focuses on systemic causes (missing alerting, unclear ownership, a gap in testing) rather than individual blame, since blame-driven postmortems make people hide mistakes instead of surfacing them.

Tools like Chaos Monkey/Gremlin intentionally kill instances, inject latency, or partition networks in production (or a production-like environment) to verify your resilience assumptions actually hold before a real outage tests them for you.

FinOps practices — right-sizing over-provisioned instances, cleaning up orphaned volumes/snapshots, using Savings Plans/Reserved Instances for steady-state load — turn cost visibility into an ongoing engineering responsibility, not just a finance afterthought.
